Name: rate_distortion_compression
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Compression: A Rate-Distortion Approach
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limits of neural network compression can be characterized using rate-distortion theory, which provides a framework for understanding the trade-off between the bits required to represent a model and the distortion induced by compression.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored mixed-precision quantization and compression techniques for neural networks. However, these approaches often rely on heuristics and empirical methods, lacking a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Our proposal distinguishes itself by providing a principled approach to neural network compression, grounded in rate-distortion theory and the minimum description length principle.
Abstract: This proposal explores the information-theoretic limits of neural network compression using rate-distortion theory. We aim to characterize the fundamental trade-off between the bits required to represent a model and the distortion induced by compression. By developing a rate-distortion framework for neural networks, we can provide a principled approach to compression, allowing for the design of more efficient and effective compression algorithms. Our approach will be grounded in the minimum description length principle and will provide a rigorous understanding of the limits of neural network compression.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments to validate our rate-distortion framework, using techniques such as quantization and pruning to compress neural networks. We will evaluate the performance of our approach using metrics such as bits-per-weight and distortion-rate curves, and compare our results to existing compression algorithms. Specifically, we will: (1) develop a rate-distortion model for neural networks, (2) design and implement compression algorithms based on our framework, and (3) evaluate the performance of our approach on benchmark datasets and models.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The main risk factors and limitations of our proposal are: (1) the complexity of developing a rigorous rate-distortion framework for neural networks, (2) the potential difficulty of optimizing compression algorithms based on our framework, and (3) the possibility that our approach may not yield significant improvements in compression efficiency compared to existing methods.
